tabitems.title: Foundations and design principles
tabitems.content:
Foundations and design principles

The NSW Design System starts with shared foundations such as typography, colour, spacing, and interaction states. These decisions create consistency so people can move through government services without relearning interface patterns each time.

When teams align on foundations, they reduce duplicated design effort and avoid fragmented user experiences across agencies. Foundations should be applied early so layout, hierarchy, and readability are stable before component selection begins.

Use foundation rules to guide emphasis and content structure, then choose components that support the user task rather than forcing content into a pattern that does not fit.

tabitems.title: Component usage and composition
tabitems.content:
Component usage and composition

Components provide tested building blocks for common interactions, including forms, navigation, alerts, and progressive disclosure. Start from the component guidance to understand intended use, limits, and accessibility notes.

Compose pages by combining compatible components in predictable patterns. Keep each component focused on one purpose, and avoid nesting interactive patterns in ways that increase cognitive load.

Where no component directly matches your need, prefer adapting an existing pattern before introducing a new one. This keeps the system coherent and easier to maintain.

tabitems.title: Accessibility and inclusive content
tabitems.content:
Accessibility and inclusive content

The Design System is built to support WCAG 2.2 AA outcomes, but accessibility depends on implementation choices in products. Teams must use semantic markup, meaningful labels, and clear focus management in addition to system styles.

Content design is part of accessibility. Write plain language instructions, keep link text specific, and ensure headings explain what users will find next.

Include accessibility testing in delivery workflows with keyboard checks, screen reader reviews, and real user testing where possible.

tabitems.title: Theming, tokens and branding
tabitems.content:
Theming, tokens and branding

Theme tokens allow teams to apply brand expression while preserving functional consistency and contrast requirements. Use token-based customisation rather than hard-coded colours or ad hoc spacing values.

Brand choices should support comprehension and trust, not compete with task completion. Maintain clear state changes for hover, focus, and active interactions across light and dark contexts.

When introducing theme variants, test them against key components and content densities to confirm readability and interaction clarity at common breakpoints.

tabitems.title: Contribution workflow and governance
tabitems.content:
Contribution workflow and governance

Changes to the Design System should be evidence-led and documented with clear rationale, impact, and migration notes. Contributions are strongest when they show tested outcomes, not just visual preference.

Use pull requests to capture implementation detail, accessibility considerations, and backward-compatibility implications for consuming products. Include examples for both standard and themed contexts where relevant.

Governance ensures the system remains stable while evolving. Review patterns regularly, retire legacy behaviour intentionally, and provide guidance so teams can adopt updates safely.
